narrative: I'm sorry to share that our admissions model predicted a 66% chance you'll struggle and fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize students likely to succeed long-term. Your profile shows solid choices like planning full-time study and coming from a middle-income family, but it highlights challenges: your undergraduate GPA of 2.6 sits below the typical 3.21 average, your LSAT score of 31.5 is lower than the usual 36.7, and your school decile ranks were quite low at 2nd for Year 1 (versus an average 5.71) and 1st for Year 3 (against 5.53 average). As a female Asian student, you're in underrepresented groups, which make up smaller shares of successful bar passers. The model's decision hinged most on your Year 1 decile rank, as that low performance strongly signaled higher bar failure risk compared to peers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which fell short and pulled the prediction toward failure rather than success. Your Year 3 decile followed closely, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic struggles relative to others. Family income level, your racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender rounded out the key factors, each nudging the odds toward failure in descending order of impact—though the top three academic metrics carried the heaviest weight. Overall, your metrics landed below averages for students who typically pass the bar, painting a picture of elevated risk. The good news is you can take action: retake the LSAT to boost that score, pursue graduate courses or a post-bac program to strengthen your GPA and decile equivalents, and seek targeted bar prep resources early. Building a stronger academic track record now could flip this prediction for future applications. You're capable of turning this around—many students do by focusing on these fixable areas. Keep pushing; your determination will make the difference.
